definition: Rigid curved device inserted through mouth to hold tongue forward
rationale: The OPA is a rigid plastic device inserted through the mouth to prevent the tongue from obstructing the pharynx. It is used in unconscious patients without a gag reflex and must be properly sized.

definition: Flexible tube inserted through nose to maintain airway
rationale: The NPA is a soft flexible tube inserted through the nostril into the nasopharynx. It is better tolerated than OPA in patients with intact gag reflex and maintains airway patency.

definition: Device used to remove secretions and fluids from airway
rationale: Suction catheters connect to suction units to remove blood vomit and secretions from the airway. Rigid catheters are used for the oropharynx while flexible catheters reach deeper.

definition: Rigid suction tip for oropharyngeal suctioning
rationale: The Yankauer or tonsil tip catheter is a rigid plastic suction device designed for oral suctioning. Its large bore and rigid construction make it effective for removing thick secretions and vomit.

definition: Manual resuscitator for delivering positive pressure ventilation
rationale: The BVM or Ambu bag is a handheld device for providing positive pressure ventilation. It consists of a self-inflating bag one-way valve and face mask. It can be used with or without supplemental oxygen.

definition: Barrier device for mouth-to-mask ventilation
rationale: The pocket mask provides a barrier between rescuer and patient during rescue breathing. It has a one-way valve and can connect to oxygen. It reduces disease transmission risk during ventilation.

definition: Pressurized container storing medical oxygen
rationale: Oxygen cylinders store compressed oxygen under high pressure. Common sizes include D portable and M large cylinders. They have regulators to control flow rate and pressure gauges to monitor contents.

definition: Device that controls oxygen flow from cylinder
rationale: The regulator attaches to the oxygen cylinder reducing high tank pressure to usable pressure and allowing adjustment of flow rate in liters per minute. It includes a pressure gauge showing remaining oxygen.

definition: Device measuring oxygen flow rate in liters per minute
rationale: The flowmeter indicates oxygen flow rate typically measured in liters per minute. It may be a floating ball or dial type. Proper flow rate selection depends on the delivery device being used.

definition: Low-flow oxygen delivery device with prongs in nostrils
rationale: The nasal cannula delivers oxygen through two small prongs inserted into the nostrils. It provides 24-44% oxygen at 1-6 LPM and is comfortable for patients who can breathe adequately.

definition: High-flow mask with reservoir delivering up to 90% oxygen
rationale: The non-rebreather mask has a reservoir bag and one-way valves allowing delivery of approximately 90% oxygen at 10-15 LPM. The reservoir must remain inflated during inspiration.

definition: Oxygen mask delivering 40-60% oxygen
rationale: The simple face mask covers nose and mouth delivering approximately 40-60% oxygen at 6-10 LPM. Flow must be at least 6 LPM to prevent CO2 rebreathing from the mask.

definition: Mask delivering precise oxygen concentrations
rationale: The Venturi mask uses color-coded adapters to deliver precise oxygen concentrations from 24-50%. It is useful for COPD patients requiring controlled oxygen delivery.

definition: Device measuring oxygen saturation non-invasively
rationale: The pulse oximeter clips to a finger or earlobe using light absorption to measure SpO2 and pulse rate. Normal SpO2 is 95-100%. It does not measure CO2 or detect carbon monoxide poisoning.

definition: Blood pressure measuring device
rationale: The sphygmomanometer consists of an inflatable cuff manometer and valve used to measure blood pressure. Manual devices require a stethoscope while automatic devices inflate and measure electronically.

definition: Instrument for listening to body sounds
rationale: The stethoscope amplifies body sounds for auscultation. It is used to hear lung sounds heart sounds blood pressure Korotkoff sounds and bowel sounds. It has a diaphragm and bell.

definition: Device that analyzes heart rhythm and delivers shocks
rationale: The AED analyzes cardiac rhythm and delivers defibrillation shocks for ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia. It provides voice prompts and is designed for use by trained laypersons and professionals.

definition: Device to stop severe extremity bleeding by compressing vessels
rationale: A tourniquet is applied proximally to a wound to occlude arterial blood flow and stop life-threatening extremity hemorrhage. Modern guidelines recommend early tourniquet use for severe bleeding.

definition: Gauze treated with agents that promote clotting
rationale: Hemostatic dressings contain agents like kaolin or chitosan that accelerate clot formation. They are used for wound packing when direct pressure alone is insufficient to control bleeding.

definition: Device to limit neck movement in suspected spine injury
rationale: The cervical collar or C-collar limits movement of the cervical spine in trauma patients. It must be properly sized and applied. It is used with manual stabilization until full immobilization is achieved.

definition: Rigid board for full spinal immobilization
rationale: The long backboard provides a rigid surface for immobilizing the entire spine. The patient is secured with straps and head blocks. It is used for suspected spinal injury with mechanism of injury.

definition: Foam blocks used to immobilize the head on a backboard
rationale: Head blocks or head immobilization devices are placed on either side of the head and secured with straps to prevent lateral head movement after the patient is on the backboard.

definition: Vest-type device for spinal immobilization during extrication
rationale: The KED is a vest-type short spine immobilization device used for seated patients requiring extrication from vehicles. It immobilizes the head neck and torso while maintaining spinal alignment during movement.

definition: Split stretcher that slides under patient without rolling
rationale: The scoop stretcher splits into two halves that are slid under the patient from each side then locked together. It allows patient pickup with minimal movement making it useful for suspected spinal injury.

definition: Device to immobilize injured extremity
rationale: Splints immobilize fractures and dislocations preventing further injury and reducing pain. Types include rigid board splints air splints traction splints and improvised splints.

definition: Device that applies pulling force to align femur fractures
rationale: Traction splints like the Hare or Sager apply longitudinal traction to femur fractures reducing pain bleeding and muscle spasm. They should not be used for hip knee or lower leg injuries.

definition: Moldable foam-covered aluminum splint
rationale: The SAM splint is a flexible foam-padded aluminum splint that can be molded to fit any extremity. Its versatility makes it useful for various fracture locations and positions.

definition: Arm support and body wrap for upper extremity immobilization
rationale: A sling supports the forearm and hand while the swathe wraps around the body to secure the arm against the chest. Together they immobilize shoulder and upper arm injuries.

definition: Primary patient transport device in ambulance
rationale: The wheeled stretcher or cot is the primary device for transporting patients in ambulances. It has wheels an adjustable frame safety rails and restraints. Patients are secured before transport.

definition: Wheeled chair for moving patients on stairs
rationale: The stair chair is a lightweight wheeled chair designed for moving patients up and down stairs where stretchers cannot navigate. It has tracks or wheels for smooth stair descent.

definition: Collapsible stretcher for patient movement
rationale: Portable stretchers are lightweight collapsible devices used to move patients from location to the ambulance stretcher. They fold for storage and can be carried by two providers.

definition: Device for measuring blood glucose level
rationale: The glucometer measures blood glucose concentration from a small blood sample. It provides rapid results to help identify hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Proper technique and calibration ensure accuracy.

definition: Device for measuring body temperature
rationale: Thermometers measure body temperature using various methods including oral tympanic temporal artery and rectal. Normal temperature is approximately 98.6°F or 37°C with normal variation.

definition: Small flashlight for pupil assessment and examination
rationale: The penlight is a small bright light used to assess pupil reaction to light and for examination in dark areas. Pupils should constrict equally and briskly when light is applied.

definition: Protective gloves made of synthetic rubber
rationale: Nitrile gloves are non-latex synthetic rubber gloves used for patient care. They provide barrier protection against blood and body fluids without latex allergy risk. They should be changed between patients.

definition: Protective device covering face from splashes
rationale: Face shields provide a barrier protecting the entire face including eyes from splashes and sprays of blood and body fluids. They are used during procedures with splash risk.

definition: Mask filtering at least 95% of airborne particles
rationale: N95 respirators filter at least 95% of airborne particles including small droplet nuclei. They require fit testing and are used for airborne disease protection such as tuberculosis.

definition: Protective garment covering clothing
rationale: Isolation gowns protect clothing and skin from contact with blood and body fluids. They are worn for procedures with significant contamination risk and are removed carefully after use.

definition: Clean absorbent dressing material
rationale: Sterile gauze pads and rolls are used to cover wounds absorb drainage and apply direct pressure for bleeding control. Sterility reduces infection risk in open wounds.

definition: Airtight dressing that prevents air passage
rationale: Occlusive dressings seal wounds from air. They are used for sucking chest wounds to prevent air entry and for burns to prevent moisture loss. Petroleum gauze and plastic wrap are examples.

definition: Bandage designed to apply continuous pressure
rationale: Pressure bandages are elastic bandages applied firmly over wounds to maintain continuous direct pressure for hemorrhage control. They free the provider's hands while maintaining pressure.

definition: Versatile cloth for slings bandages and padding
rationale: The triangular bandage is a versatile piece of cloth that can be used as a sling bandage pressure dressing or padding. It can be folded into a cravat for various applications.

definition: Long strip of gauze for wrapping wounds
rationale: Roller bandages are long rolls of gauze used to secure dressings wrap injured areas and provide light compression. They come in various widths for different body parts.

definition: Pre-packaged supplies for emergency childbirth
rationale: The OB kit contains supplies for emergency delivery including sterile gloves drapes umbilical clamps scissors bulb syringe blankets and cord clamps. It prepares providers for field delivery.

definition: Device for suctioning infant airway
rationale: The bulb syringe is a handheld suction device used to clear mucus and fluid from a newborn's mouth and nose. The bulb is compressed before insertion then released to create suction.

definition: Devices to clamp the umbilical cord after cutting
rationale: Umbilical clamps are placed on the umbilical cord to stop blood flow before the cord is cut. Two clamps are placed several inches apart and the cord is cut between them.

definition: Handheld two-way communication device
rationale: Portable radios allow EMS providers to communicate with dispatch hospitals and other units. They operate on designated frequencies and are essential for coordinating emergency response.

definition: Computer in vehicle for receiving dispatch information
rationale: The MDT is a computer in the emergency vehicle that receives dispatch information displays call details provides mapping and allows electronic documentation. It enhances communication efficiency.

definition: Pre-filled device for emergency epinephrine injection
rationale: Epinephrine auto-injectors like EpiPen contain pre-measured epinephrine and automatically inject when pressed against the thigh. They are used for anaphylaxis and are designed for emergency use.

definition: Device delivering measured medication doses for inhalation
rationale: MDIs deliver precise doses of aerosolized medication for respiratory conditions. Patients press down to release medication while inhaling. A spacer can improve medication delivery to the lungs.
